items.headline: HKEX Greenlights Treasury Share Buybacks Without Cancellation
items.whatsTheNews: Hong Kong's stock exchange (HKEX) has changed its rules so listed companies can now keep the shares they buy back instead of cancelling them. They can hold these shares in reserve and resell them into the market later. The new regime took effect this month after a public consultation.
items.whatItMeans: A share buyback is when a company uses its own cash to buy its shares back off the stock market. Until now, Hong Kong forced companies to cancel those shares, so they disappeared for good. "Treasury shares" are bought-back shares the company keeps in reserve instead of cancelling — think of it as putting them in a drawer to reuse later. The change hands companies a flexible pool of their own shares they can sell again whenever they need cash.
clients: Raising cash gets easier. If a company needs money, it can resell shares it already owns instead of going through the slow, expensive process of issuing new shares. That saves it time and legal fees.
It helps them pay staff in shares. Many companies reward senior employees with shares; now they can hand over ones they already hold rather than printing new ones.
But there are traps. Selling shares back into the market can lower the value of everyone else's shares (called dilution), and there are strict rules on when and how to do it — so they need a lawyer.
lawFirms: It creates steady, repeat work. Every buyback or resale needs lawyers to draft documents and check the rules, so the capital markets team gets a reliable flow of billable work.
It rewards firms strong in equity work. Firms with big "equity capital markets" teams are the natural first call, so being good at this wins the whole client relationship.
More work means more hiring. A busy team needs juniors and trainees to handle the paperwork — exactly the kind of seat a vacation scheme student could sit in.

items.headline: Hong Kong Passes Its First Stablecoin Licensing Regime
items.whatsTheNews: Hong Kong has passed a law requiring anyone who issues a stablecoin to first get a licence from the Hong Kong Monetary Authority (HKMA). Issuers must fully back their coins with real reserves and meet strict anti-money-laundering standards. The HKMA has said it will grant only a handful of licences at the start.
items.whatItMeans: A stablecoin is a kind of digital money designed to always be worth the same as a real currency — for example, one coin always equals one US dollar. The HKMA is essentially Hong Kong's central bank, the body that oversees money and banking. "Backing coins with reserves" means an issuer must hold enough real money to repay everyone who owns a coin. This makes Hong Kong one of the first places in the world with clear rules for this type of digital money.
clients: Companies finally know the rules. Before, firms were nervous about launching digital coins because the law was unclear. Now there is a clear path, so more will try — and they need lawyers to apply.
The requirements are tough. Each company must prove it holds enough reserve money and has strong anti-fraud systems, so they need legal and compliance help to get it right.
Mistakes are costly. Operating without a licence, or breaking the rules, can lead to heavy penalties — so clients will happily pay for advice to stay safe.
lawFirms: A brand-new area of law just opened. It sits between banking, payments, and crypto, so firms can build a fresh practice and win clients with nowhere else to turn yet.
The work keeps coming. Once licensed, a client needs ongoing advice to stay compliant — long-term income, not a one-off job.
Early movers win big. Firms that learn this first become the go-to experts, and that reputation pulls in the biggest clients and the most interesting work for juniors.

items.headline: Mainland Mega-IPOs Return to Hong Kong's Listing Pipeline
items.whatsTheNews: A wave of large mainland Chinese companies have filed to list in Hong Kong, including major consumer brands and battery makers raising billions of dollars. Hong Kong is on track for one of its strongest years for IPO proceeds in over a decade. Chinese regulators have been clearing companies already listed on the mainland to also list in Hong Kong.
items.whatItMeans: An IPO ("initial public offering") is when a private company sells its shares to the public for the first time and becomes listed on a stock exchange — companies do this to raise large amounts of money from investors. A "secondary listing" is when a company already listed in one market lists its shares in a second one too, which is what these mainland firms are doing by adding a Hong Kong listing. More big listings means a lot of high-value legal work flowing into the city.
clients: Mainland companies reach global money. Listing in Hong Kong lets them raise funds from international investors in a currency that is easy to use worldwide.
They face two rulebooks at once. They must satisfy both the mainland regulator and the Hong Kong exchange, so they need lawyers who understand both systems.
Accuracy is everything. An IPO involves huge amounts of disclosure, and one mistake can sink the deal — so they lean heavily on their advisers.
lawFirms: IPOs are among the biggest money-makers for firms. One large listing means months of work and big teams, so a busy pipeline directly boosts revenue.
Cross-border skill decides who wins. Firms with real teams in Hong Kong and mainland China — and Mandarin-speaking lawyers — land the largest deals.
It drives trainee hiring. IPO work needs lots of junior lawyers, so a full pipeline means more trainees. Good news if you are applying.

items.headline: Hong Kong Court Backs Worldwide Freezing Order in Crypto Fraud
items.whatsTheNews: A Hong Kong court has granted a worldwide freezing order over assets linked to a cryptocurrency fraud. The court treated the stolen digital coins as property the law can protect and recover. It even allowed the legal papers to be served by sending them straight to the fraudster's digital wallet.
items.whatItMeans: A "freezing order" is a court order that stops someone moving or hiding their money while a case is going on, and "worldwide" means it covers their assets in any country, not just Hong Kong. Treating crypto as "property" matters because the law gives strong protection to property — so victims can chase stolen coins much like they would chase stolen cash. Serving documents to a digital wallet is a creative workaround for when you cannot identify or find the thief in person. Together it shows Hong Kong courts are willing to use modern tools to fight digital fraud.
clients: Fraud victims get real protection. The court can freeze stolen digital money before the thief hides it, giving victims a genuine chance of getting it back.
Crypto businesses feel safer here. Treating digital coins as real property makes Hong Kong a more trusted place to operate.
They need specialist help fast. Recovering crypto is technical and urgent, so victims pay for litigators who understand court orders and how to trace money on the blockchain.
lawFirms: A fast-growing type of court work. Crypto disputes keep rising, so litigation teams that handle them stay busy.
It needs teamwork with tech experts. Lawyers work with blockchain investigators to trace the money, which makes the work high-value.
It pulls international work to Hong Kong. Because courts grant these orders, more overseas clients bring their disputes here — meaning more work for HK firms.

items.headline: SFC Tightens Disclosure Rules for Crypto Trading Platforms
items.whatsTheNews: The Securities and Futures Commission (SFC) has brought in new rules forcing licensed crypto trading platforms to spell out the risks and explain how they store customers' coins. Platforms must change how they sign up new customers within a set deadline. The rules follow a string of complaints from people who lost money.
items.whatItMeans: The SFC is Hong Kong's main financial markets watchdog — the regulator that polices investments and the firms that sell them. "Retail users" means ordinary, everyday investors, as opposed to big professional institutions. "Custody" refers to how a platform looks after and safeguards the coins its customers own. The rules are aimed at protecting beginners who may not realise how risky crypto can be.
clients: Platforms have urgent work to do. They must rewrite their risk warnings and customer agreements before the deadline, so they need lawyers quickly.
Getting it wrong is dangerous. If a platform's disclosures fall short, it can be punished or sued, so clients want advice to protect themselves.
It changes their daily operations. The rules reshape the sign-up process, so platforms need ongoing help to stay compliant.
lawFirms: Short-term project work appears immediately. Helping clients update documents before a deadline is exactly what regulatory teams pick up.
It can become long-term work. After the fix, clients often keep lawyers on to check they stay compliant — meaning repeat income.
It rewards a mix of skills. Firms that understand both finance rules and consumer protection are best placed to win this work.

items.headline: Global Minimum Tax Starts to Bite Hong Kong Multinationals
items.whatsTheNews: Large multinational companies based in Hong Kong are paying under the new global minimum tax for the first time, and Hong Kong has brought in its own "top-up" tax to match. Groups that previously paid below 15% may now owe extra. These are the first filings under the new international rules.
items.whatItMeans: The global minimum tax is a worldwide agreement to make big multinational companies pay at least 15% tax, no matter where they are based. It was designed to stop companies shifting their profits into very low-tax places just to avoid paying. A "top-up tax" is the extra amount charged to lift a company's rate up to 15% if it was paying less. Hong Kong has traditionally low taxes, so this hits big groups headquartered here directly.
clients: Many big companies will pay more. If a company's tax rate was below 15%, it now has to top it up, which directly costs money — so it wants advice on exactly how much it owes.
The paperwork is heavy and new. These rules require detailed reporting companies have never done before, so they need tax lawyers and accountants to handle it.
Some will restructure. Companies may reorganise how they are set up to manage the impact, and that reorganisation needs legal advice.
lawFirms: Tax teams get a wave of new work. Working out what clients owe, helping them report correctly, and handling disputes all create billable hours.
It rewards firms with global reach. These rules cross borders, so firms that can coordinate across countries are the most useful to clients.
Disputes will follow. As the rules settle in, companies and tax authorities will argue over the details — giving tax and litigation teams even more work later.
